column2:
Patanjali

~2nd c. BCE

Indian / Yoga

Patanjali synthesis

The Yoga Sutras establish the most rigorous classical dualism: puruṣa (consciousness) and prakṛti (matter-energy) are two eternal, categorically distinct principles, and their apparent association is the root of all suffering. Consciousness is the pure witness — featureless, ownerless, never an object of any act — while everything ordinarily called the mind (thoughts, emotions, memories, the sense of self) belongs entirely to prakṛti. Liberation is not an achievement but a recognition: consciousness was always already free, always already uninvolved in the world it witnesses. The Yoga Sutras map the practical discipline — eight limbs, five vṛttis, the spectrum of samādhi from gross absorption to seedless silence — by which this recognition is earned.
column3:
Vasubandhu

4th–5th c. CE

Indian / Buddhist

Vasubandhu synthesis

Vijñaptimātratā — representation-only — is Vasubandhu's radical thesis: consciousness is all there is, and what appears as an external world is vijñapti, representation arising from within the ālayavijñāna. The eight vijñānas (five sensory, mano-vijñāna, manas, ālaya) constitute a complete architecture of mind, from surface sensation to the deep storehouse carrying karmic seeds across lifetimes. The self is the seventh vijñāna's perpetual misidentification of the ālaya as a permanent subject — structurally generated, not accidental — dissolved by wisdom that sees the ālaya for what it is. Liberation transforms all eight vijñānas into the four buddha-wisdoms; the Vimśatikā and Triṃśikā provide the logical demonstration.
column4:
Xuanzang

602–664 CE

Chinese / Buddhist

Xuanzang synthesis

The Cheng Weishi Lun is the definitive Yogācāra synthesis: Xuanzang integrates divergent lineages, resolves contradictions between Vasubandhu's commentators, and adds original analyses unmatched by any predecessor. His most significant contribution is the five-mode analysis of mano-vijñāna — operating in conjunction with the senses, in dreaming, in meditative absorption, in unconscious states, and at death — giving a more complete taxonomy of altered and transitional states of consciousness than any earlier text. The āśraya distinction (body as support-condition, not cause, of sensory consciousness) lets him account for the effects of sense-organ damage while maintaining that consciousness arises from seeds, not matter. The path of liberation is the progressive transformation of all eight vijñānas into wisdom, ending in the mirror-like awareness of a fully awakened mind.
column5:
Abhinavagupta

~950–1016 CE

Kashmiri / Shaiva

Abhinavagupta synthesis

Kashmir Śaivism at its most philosophically ambitious: consciousness is not a property of anything but the absolute itself — Śiva as pure self-luminous awareness (prakāśa) knowing itself in an act of self-recognition (vimarśa). The thirty-six tattvas map consciousness's self-contraction from the absolute to the atom; the individual ego is a contracted form of the absolute aham, and liberation is recognition (pratyabhijñā) that this very experience, just as it is, is Śiva's self-awareness. The body is not an obstacle but consciousness at its densest — flesh is frozen light — making tantric practice a transformation from within rather than an escape. Aesthetics is inseparable from philosophy: in genuine rasa-experience the ego dissolves and pure qualitative luminosity remains, which is identical in structure to liberation.
column6:
Ibn Sīnā

980–1037 CE

Persian / Islamic

Ibn Sīnā synthesis

The Flying Man argument is Ibn Sīnā's central contribution: a subject suspended in void, deprived of all sensation and bodily awareness, would still know with certainty that it exists — demonstrating that the soul's self-knowledge is immediate, prior to any bodily evidence, and therefore that the soul is not identical with or dependent on the body. The soul uses the body as an instrument (vegetative, animal, and rational faculties in ascending order), but its highest cognitive act — intellectual intuition of universal forms — requires no bodily organ and is the soul's participation in the Active Intellect, the cosmic principle of pure thought. Death is the soul's separation from its instrument, not its destruction: the soul is simple, and simple things cannot disintegrate. All of Ibn Sīnā's accounts of qualia, self, time, and intersubjectivity follow from this core claim about the soul's radical self-sufficiency.
column7:
Leibniz

1646–1716 CE

German

Leibniz synthesis

Every element of Leibniz's account of consciousness follows from a single principle: each simple substance (monad) perceives the entire universe from its unique perspective, with the grade of consciousness determined by the clarity of that perception. The infinite gradient from sleeping monads (bare unconscious perception) through animal consciousness to human apperception to divine omniscience is the first modern theory of the unconscious and the most continuous account of levels in this table. Pre-established harmony replaces causal interaction: soul and body correspond perfectly because God synchronized them at creation — no real exchange passes between them, yet the correspondence is exact. The monad has no windows, its entire history unfolding from its internal law of appetition, yet it mirrors all other monads; God alone perceives all perspectives simultaneously and with perfect clarity.
column8:
William James

1842–1910 CE

American

William James synthesis

The stream of consciousness — continuous, flowing, selective, owned, always already meaningful — is James's central metaphor and a precise phenomenological claim against the atomism of British empiricism. The fringe expands qualitative experience to include the felt quality of relations, transitions, and tendencies; the specious present makes temporal depth intrinsic to every moment rather than added by memory. His theory of emotion (we feel afraid because we tremble) makes consciousness irreducibly somatic; his analysis of selective attention makes it irreducibly interested, purposive, and biological. The Varieties of Religious Experience extends the analysis to extraordinary states, arguing that mystical consciousness has noetic authority that ordinary consciousness cannot dismiss — and that the boundaries of the individual self may be more permeable than common sense assumes.
column9:
Husserl

1859–1938 CE

German / Czech

Husserl synthesis

Intentionality — every act of consciousness is an act of something — is the founding discovery of phenomenology, worked out through the noesis-noema correlation: the intending act constitutes the meaning of its object, which is neither a real external thing nor a mental image but the meaning-structure through which objects are given. The epoché (bracketing the natural attitude) reveals what ordinary science takes for granted: inner time-consciousness (retention, primal impression, protention), the lived body (Leib) as the zero-point of all spatial orientation and the medium of worldly engagement, the constitution of other egos through analogical apperception. The transcendental ego is not a substance but the formal pole from which all acts flow and to which all acts refer — neither a Cartesian soul nor an empirical person. Transcendental phenomenology does not explain consciousness from outside but articulates its own self-giving structures from within, making consciousness the presupposition of all inquiry that cannot itself be made into a mere object.
column10:
Edelman

1929–2014 CE

American

Edelman synthesis

The Theory of Neuronal Group Selection (TNGS) is the most thoroughgoing biological account of consciousness in this table: consciousness arises from reentrant signaling between the thalamocortical system and other brain regions, forming a dynamic core whose coalition shifts moment by moment — not a computation but a biological process continuously shaped by the organism's evolutionary history of values. The distinction between primary consciousness (real-time construction of a mental scene, shared by many animals) and higher-order consciousness (self-model, language, explicit temporal depth — a qualitative divide, not merely quantitative) is Edelman's central contribution. Qualia are biological values — the experiential face of what evolution has built the organism to care about — which makes them real without making them mysterious. There is no homunculus, no Cartesian theater; consciousness is an organ of a living body, and it cannot exist in a brain in a vat.
column11:
Chalmers

1966– CE

Australian / American

Chalmers synthesis

The hard problem — why any physical or functional process is accompanied by subjective experience at all — is Chalmers's central contribution, and its force is logical: even a complete functional description of a system leaves it entirely open whether there is something it is like to instantiate it. The zombie argument makes this precise: a being physically and functionally identical to a human but lacking any inner experience is logically conceivable, which shows that phenomenal consciousness cannot be logically entailed by physical facts. His taxonomy — easy problems (attention, memory, access) vs. the hard problem — reveals that most of what cognitive science explains is not what is most philosophically puzzling. His openness to panpsychism — phenomenal properties as fundamental features of reality, present wherever information is processed — places him unexpectedly in the neighborhood of Leibniz and Abhinavagupta, reached by logical analysis rather than metaphysical first principles.

column1:
Nature

Substance, process, or field?

Nature synthesis

Ten thinkers, five fundamental positions. Patanjali: consciousness is the pure witness (puruṣa), utterly distinct from matter. Vasubandhu and Xuanzang: consciousness-only — there is no external reality outside mind. Abhinavagupta: consciousness is the absolute (Śiva), the ground of all existence. Ibn Sīnā, Leibniz, James, Husserl defend a real but non-ultimate subject — immaterial soul, monadic perception, stream, or intentional field. Edelman: consciousness is a late-arriving biological process. Chalmers: whatever it is, phenomenal consciousness is irreducible to any physical or functional description.
column2:
Consciousness as the pure witness — puruṣa

For Patanjali, consciousness is *puruṣa* — the pure witness, the unchanging spectator. It is not a process, not an event, not a product of matter. Puruṣa is the only thing that truly sees; everything else — including the mind (*citta*), the intellect (*buddhi*), even the most refined states of meditation — belongs to *prakṛti*, the domain of matter-energy in its many transformations. Consciousness does not think, feel, or perceive in the ordinary sense: it *illuminates*. The entire Yoga Sutras rest on this razor-sharp distinction: consciousness is what gives experience its luminosity, but it is itself beyond experience. The fundamental problem of human life is that puruṣa has mistakenly identified itself with the fluctuations of the mind — *citta-vṛtti* — and yoga is the systematic practice of undoing that identification. Nature is not the cause of consciousness; consciousness is the condition that makes nature's products appear.
column3:
Consciousness-only — vijñaptimātratā

Vasubandhu's answer to the nature of consciousness is radical and uncompromising: *vijñaptimātratā* — representation-only, or consciousness-only. There is no external physical world independent of consciousness. What we take to be an outer world of objects is in fact a series of *vijñapti* — cognitive representations arising from within consciousness itself, specifically from the *ālayavijñāna*, the storehouse consciousness that carries the seeds of all past experience. The *Viṃśatikā* systematically defeats every argument for the existence of an external world: the apparent regularity of shared experience, the apparent resistance of objects, the apparent causal efficacy of the physical — all can be accounted for without positing anything outside consciousness. Nature is not separate from mind; it is mind's own internal projection, as structured and as vivid as a dream, and no more independently real.
column4:
Eight consciousnesses — vijñāna transforming vijñāna

Xuanzang synthesizes the Yogācāra tradition into a definitive account: consciousness is not a single thing but a system of eight *vijñānas* (consciousnesses) in dynamic transformation. The five sensory consciousnesses receive raw phenomenal input. The sixth — *mano-vijñāna* — is the integrating mental consciousness that combines, conceptualizes, and judges. The seventh — *manas* — is the subtle ego-sense that perpetually grasps the eighth consciousness as a self and generates the illusion of a permanent subject. The eighth — *ālayavijñāna*, the storehouse consciousness — is the deep substratum that carries all karmic seeds, matures them, and produces the appearance of a world. The *Cheng Weishi Lun* specifies: the nature of consciousness is transformation (*pariṇāma*). Consciousness is never a static substance but an ongoing process of self-differentiation, in which each of the eight vijñānas transforms the others while being transformed by them.
column5:
Consciousness as the absolute — Śiva as cit

For Abhinavagupta, consciousness (*cit*) is not a feature of the mind or a product of matter: it is the absolute itself. The Kashmir Śaiva system he synthesizes in the *Tantrāloka* identifies ultimate reality with pure self-luminous consciousness — Śiva — which freely contracts itself into the apparent multiplicity of finite minds and objects through its own sovereign power of self-concealment (*māyā*). Consciousness is not something that exists within reality; reality exists within consciousness. Every object, every sensation, every thought is a self-expression of this one absolute awareness, which recognizes itself in and through every finite form. The key term is *vimarśa* — the self-reflexive awareness that is inseparable from pure luminosity (*prakāśa*). Light without self-awareness is not truly consciousness; true consciousness is always already self-aware, and that self-awareness is identical with being itself.
column6:
The Flying Man — self-presence irreducible to body

Ibn Sīnā's answer to the nature of consciousness is his most original philosophical contribution: consciousness is self-presence, and self-presence is irreducible to any bodily or sensory state. The Flying Man thought experiment in *Kitāb al-Shifāʾ* demonstrates this with stunning economy: suppose a fully formed human being is created in mid-air, unable to see, hear, or feel anything — deprived of all sensory input and all awareness of their own body. Such a person would still know that *they exist*. The 'I' that knows itself is not a product of sensation, not derived from the body's states, not constructed from memory or perception. It is primitive, immediate, and certain in a way that nothing else is. Consciousness, for Ibn Sīnā, is this irreducible self-presence — the soul's immediate acquaintance with its own existing.
column7:
Perception as a matter of degree — petites perceptions

For Leibniz, consciousness is not a binary state — either present or absent — but a matter of degree. His central contribution to the nature of consciousness is the distinction between *petites perceptions* (minute, unconscious perceptions) and *apperception* (self-conscious awareness). Every monad perceives the entire universe from its own perspective — but most of this perception is below the threshold of awareness, a vast unconscious hum that constitutes the background of all experience. What we call consciousness is the surface of an enormous depth. The roar of the sea is composed of the imperceptible sounds of each individual wave; the felt continuity of experience is composed of infinitely many sub-threshold perceptual events. Consciousness is therefore continuous with nature, not opposed to it — it is nature becoming aware of itself by degrees, with human apperception at the highest known grade.
column8:
The stream — consciousness as flux, not substance

William James's foundational contribution is the metaphor and the argument of the *stream of consciousness* — set out in Chapter IX of the *Principles of Psychology* (1890) with a precision that belies its literary power. Consciousness is not a thing, not a substance, not a collection of discrete mental atoms. It is a *stream*: continuous, flowing, ever-changing, always already moving before we can grasp it. James identifies five marks of the stream: every thought is personal, belonging to a particular thinker; thought is always changing; thought is sensibly continuous; it always appears to deal with objects independent of itself; and it selectively attends to some aspects of its objects while ignoring others. The last of these — selective attention — is for James the most important: consciousness is not a passive mirror but an active selector, and what it selects is shaped by interest, habit, and purpose. Consciousness is not a noun; it is a verb.
column9:
Intentional structure — consciousness is always of something

For Husserl, the fundamental nature of consciousness is *intentionality*: every act of consciousness is directed toward an object. There is no consciousness in general, no pure awareness floating free — every experience is an experience *of* something, whether a perceived tree, a remembered face, an imagined sound, or an abstract mathematical object. This directedness is not a relation between two independently existing things (a mind and its object); it is the very structure of the conscious act itself. Husserl's innovation over Brentano, from whom he inherits the concept, is to distinguish rigorously between the *noesis* (the act of intending) and the *noema* (the intended content as such) — the object as it is meant, which is neither the real object in the world nor a mental image but the meaning-structure through which the object is given to consciousness. Consciousness is, at its most fundamental, a meaning-constituting activity.
column10:
Dynamic core — consciousness as reentrant neural process

For Edelman, consciousness is a biological process — specifically, the product of *reentrant signaling* between the thalamocortical system and other brain regions forming a *dynamic core*. This is not a metaphor but a precise neuroanatomical claim: consciousness arises when neuronal groups distributed across the brain engage in massively parallel, reciprocal signaling that integrates diverse sensory and motor information into a unified, moment-by-moment scene. The dynamic core is not a fixed structure but a constantly shifting coalition of neural activity, different from moment to moment, sustained by the reentrant connections that allow distant regions to speak to each other simultaneously. Edelman's key insistence is that this process is *not computational*: the brain is not a digital computer running programs, and consciousness is not software. It is a Darwinian process — neural groups compete for selection, and the winners constitute the conscious moment.
column11:
Phenomenal consciousness — irreducible to function

Chalmers draws the most influential contemporary distinction in consciousness studies: between *phenomenal consciousness* and *access consciousness*. Phenomenal consciousness is what it is *like* to be in a mental state — the redness of red, the painfulness of pain, the felt quality of any experience. Access consciousness is the functional availability of information for reasoning, reporting, and behavioral control. Chalmers argues that all the 'easy problems' of consciousness — explaining attention, memory, cognitive integration, verbal report — are problems about access consciousness and are in principle solvable by neuroscience and cognitive science. The *Hard Problem* is why any physical or functional process gives rise to phenomenal consciousness at all. Even a complete functional description of the brain would leave it entirely open why there is subjective experience rather than mere information processing in the dark. The nature of consciousness is, for Chalmers, irreducibly phenomenal — and that irreducibility is the central fact any theory must confront.

column1:
Qualia

Irreducible subjective character?

Qualia synthesis

The hard split is between those who find no explanatory gap and those for whom it is the central problem. Patanjali, Abhinavagupta, and Ibn Sīnā locate qualia within their metaphysics — pure illumination of samādhi, the absolute's self-recognition in contracted form, the soul's incorrigible inner sense — and find no residual mystery. Leibniz reduces qualia to compressed infinities; Vasubandhu and Xuanzang to ripening seeds; Edelman to biological values. James expands qualia to include the felt quality of relations and transitions. Only Chalmers insists the explanatory gap is principled: no functional or physical account can explain why there is something it is like to have any experience at all.
column2:
The quality of samādhi — direct experience beyond concept

Patanjali does not use the word 'qualia' but his entire system is organized around the problem they name. The deepest levels of *samādhi* — meditative absorption — are defined precisely by what remains when conceptual overlay is stripped away: *savitarka* and *nirvitarka samādhi* distinguish between absorption with conceptual content and absorption without it, arriving finally at the direct acquaintance with the object as it is, prior to naming or categorizing. This bare phenomenal presence — the object shining in its own nature, unfiltered by mental interpretation — is for Patanjali the purest form of empirical knowledge. And beneath even this lies *nirbīja samādhi*: absorption without any object, pure luminous witnessing. The qualitative character of experience is not a philosophical puzzle for Patanjali but the very thing yoga is designed to purify, thin out, and ultimately dissolve — not because qualia are unreal but because they bind the witness to the world of change.
column3:
Seeds of experience — qualia as karmic residue

For Vasubandhu, the qualitative character of experience — what it is like to see blue, to feel hunger, to be afraid — arises from the maturation of *bījas* (seeds) stored in the ālayavijñāna. Every past experience deposits a seed; seeds ripen into present experience; present experience deposits new seeds. The qualitative texture of what arises is determined by the accumulated karmic history of the stream of consciousness. There is no external world whose properties the mind passively receives; the richness and specificity of phenomenal experience — its colors, textures, tastes, pains — is entirely the inner flowering of what was previously planted. This makes qualia real — they are not illusions — but it denies them any grounding in an independently existing material world. Their reality is the reality of consciousness itself, which is the only reality there is.
column4:
Transformation of seeds into phenomenal appearance

Xuanzang's *Cheng Weishi Lun* provides the most technically elaborate pre-modern account of how qualitative experience arises. The ālayavijñāna contains seeds of two kinds: seeds of the vijñānas (the cognitive consciousnesses) and seeds of dharmas (experiential properties). When conditions are ripe, seeds of the sensory vijñānas activate and give rise to the qualitative character of sensory experience — the specific redness, the specific painfulness, the specific sweetness that constitutes a moment of experience. Crucially, Xuanzang argues that the apparent external object and the experiencing subject arise simultaneously from the same ālayavijñāna in a single transformative moment: there is no prior external object causing the experience and no prior subject receiving it. Both the qualitative content and the sense of a self that has it are co-products of the same underlying process. Qualia are therefore neither subjective (in the Cartesian sense of belonging to a pre-existing self) nor objective (in the realist sense of mirroring an independent world): they are the self-display of consciousness transforming itself.
column5:
Rasa and vimarśa — qualia as self-recognition of the absolute

Abhinavagupta's account of qualia is the most philosophically ambitious in this table: qualitative experience is not a puzzle about how physical processes give rise to something subjective, because there is no physical process that is not already a form of consciousness. The specific quality of an experience — its *rasa*, its felt texture, its aesthetic and emotional character — is the absolute consciousness recognizing itself in a particular contracted form. This is why aesthetic experience (*rasa* in its classical Sanskrit sense) holds a privileged position in Abhinavagupta's system: in the moment of pure aesthetic absorption, the ordinary sense of a separate self dissolves, and what remains is the pure qualitative luminosity of the experience itself — which is nothing other than Śiva's own self-recognition. The Hard Problem, in this framework, does not arise: there is no explanatory gap between the physical and the phenomenal because the physical is already phenomenal all the way down.
column6:
Inner sense — the soul's direct acquaintance with its own states

Ibn Sīnā distinguishes sharply between the soul's knowledge of external objects (which is mediated, fallible, and dependent on the body's sense organs) and its knowledge of its own states (which is immediate, direct, and infallible). The qualitative character of inner experience — the painfulness of pain, the pleasurableness of pleasure, the specific feeling-tone of an emotion — is given to the soul by *inner sense* (*al-ḥiss al-mushtarak* and its higher analogs) in a way that requires no inference and admits no error. The soul does not deduce that it is in pain; it simply finds itself there. This direct acquaintance is a form of knowledge that Ibn Sīnā takes to be among the most certain available to us — more certain, in fact, than any knowledge of external objects. The richness of qualitative experience is thus for him evidence of the soul's non-material nature: a purely material thing could register causal impacts but could not be immediately present to itself as a specific felt quality.
column7:
Quality as confused perception — the felt texture of infinite detail

Leibniz's account of qualia is one of his most original contributions and one of the least recognized. The specific qualitative character of an experience — the redness of red, the warmth of warmth — is for him a *confused perception*: a perception that represents an enormous multitude of minute distinctions without being able to distinguish them clearly. When I see red, I am in fact perceiving the rapid vibrations of a certain kind of matter, the reflection of specific wavelengths, the activation of retinal cells — but I perceive all of this as a single undivided quality because the perception is too fine-grained for distinct representation. Color, sound, taste, smell — all qualitative experience is an analogical blur of infinite microscopic detail, compressed into a single felt tone. This does not make qualia unreal or eliminable; it makes them the signature of a finite perceiving perspective that cannot fully articulate what it dimly grasps. Every quale is an abbreviated infinity.
column8:
The fringe — felt quality as the penumbra of thought

James's contribution to the theory of qualia is the concept of the *fringe* — one of the most underappreciated ideas in the history of consciousness. The substantive parts of thought (vivid, focused, nameable) are surrounded by a felt fringe of relations, tendencies, and transitive movements that give thinking its sense of direction, coherence, and meaning. The feeling that a sentence is moving toward its conclusion, the felt recognition that a word is on the tip of the tongue, the sense of familiarity that precedes identification — all of these are qualitative experiences, but they are experiences of *relations* rather than objects. James insists that relations are as much a part of immediate experience as the things they relate — which means that the qualitative character of experience is far richer, more continuous, and more structurally complex than the simple 'sense data' of the British empiricists. Qualia for James are not the atomistic building blocks of experience but its living texture, saturated with felt direction and felt meaning.
column9:
Hyletic data — the raw sensory material of intentional acts

Husserl's account of qualia operates through his distinction between *hyletic data* (the raw sensory material) and the *intentional morphe* (the animating meaning-structure that transforms raw sensation into the experience of an object). The redness I see is not just a datum — it is redness-as-the-color-of-this-apple, shaped by the intentional act that gives it its specific sense. But beneath the intentional act lies the hyletic stratum: the brute sensory quality that is neither already interpreted nor a mere physical event, but a pre-objective felt presence. Husserl refuses both to eliminate qualia into functional processes and to detach them from their intentional context. The qualitative character of experience is real, irreducible, and essential to the structure of consciousness — but it is always already embedded in a meaning-giving act that determines *which* quality appears and as *what*.
column10:
Qualia as biological values — the evolutionary ground of feeling

Edelman's account of qualia is unique in this table for being simultaneously a defense of their reality and a fully naturalistic explanation of their origin. Qualia are real — Edelman is emphatic on this point and critical of eliminativist denials — but they are real as biological values. Every qualitative dimension of experience — pain, pleasure, color, warmth — corresponds to a value system that evolution has built into the nervous system: the reward signals, the aversive signals, the homeostatic gradients that guide behavior toward survival and reproduction. The specific felt quality of pain is not an arbitrary byproduct of neural processing; it is the signature of a system that has learned, over millions of years, that tissue damage demands immediate withdrawal. Qualia are thus the experiential face of biological value: the felt urgency of what matters to an organism constituted by evolutionary history. To explain qualia is not to explain them away; it is to show that their existence is precisely what we should expect from a nervous system shaped by natural selection.
column11:
The hard problem — qualia as the explanatory gap

Chalmers's contribution to the theory of qualia is to give philosophical precision to what everyone dimly senses but struggles to articulate: the fact that no functional or structural account of the brain tells us why there is *something it is like* to be in a neural state rather than nothing. He names this the *explanatory gap* and argues that it is not merely a gap in current knowledge but a principled obstacle: even a complete physics and neuroscience, specifying every causal-functional role in the brain, would leave untouched the question of why those processes are accompanied by experience. The zombie argument crystallizes this: it is conceivable (and Chalmers argues logically possible) that there could exist a being physically and functionally identical to a human being with no inner experience whatsoever. If that is conceivable, then phenomenal consciousness cannot be logically entailed by physical facts alone. Qualia are therefore not identical to functional states; they are something over and above them — the most mysterious and important fact about the universe.

column1:
Levels

Graded or all-or-nothing?

Levels synthesis

Three models emerge. The ontological-descent model (Abhinavagupta's 36 tattvas, Ibn Sīnā's vegetative-animal-rational hierarchy, Leibniz's sleeping-to-rational gradient) treats levels as stages of contraction or elevation within a single reality. The functional-stratification model (Vasubandhu's and Xuanzang's eight vijñānas, Edelman's primary vs. higher-order consciousness, Patanjali's samādhi spectrum) treats levels as distinct cognitive systems with their own objects and modes. The phenomenological-structure model (James's fringe-and-focus, Husserl's pre-reflective/reflective and triple temporal structure, Chalmers's easy/hard taxonomy) reveals internal differentiation within any given moment of experience rather than a vertical hierarchy to ascend.
column2:
Eight limbs, five fluctuations, three states — a complete cartography

Patanjali provides the most systematic pre-modern taxonomy of levels of consciousness. The *citta-vṛttis* — fluctuations of the mind-field — are of five types: valid cognition, error, conceptual construction, sleep, and memory. These constitute the active surface of ordinary consciousness. Beneath and above them lies the spectrum of *samādhi*: from gross absorption with conceptual content (*savitarka*) through increasingly refined levels of objectless absorption to the highest state of *nirbīja samādhi* — seedless absorption in which no object, no thought, and finally no sense of a meditator remains. The five *kliśṭa vṛttis* (afflicted fluctuations) generate suffering; the five *akliśṭa vṛttis* lead toward liberation. The progression through the eight limbs of yoga is a systematic ascent through levels of consciousness, and the *Yoga Sutras* chart this ascent with a precision unmatched in any other ancient text.
column3:
Five sensory, mano, manas, ālaya — eight layers of mind

Vasubandhu's account of levels of consciousness is structured by the eight vijñāna system. The five sensory consciousnesses operate at the most surface level: they receive the raw phenomenal input of sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch, and they are the most obviously tied to the present moment. The sixth — mano-vijñāna — operates at the conceptual level, combining sensory inputs, forming judgments, planning, remembering, imagining. The seventh — manas — is a subtler and more insidious level: it perpetually misidentifies the ālayavijñāna as a permanent self, generating the fundamental ego-illusion that underlies all suffering. The eighth — ālayavijñāna — is the deepest level, operating below the threshold of ordinary awareness, carrying karmic seeds, maturing them, and supporting the continuity of the stream of consciousness across time and across rebirths. These are not metaphorical layers but distinct cognitive processes, each with its own object, its own mode of knowing, and its own relation to the others.
column4:
Primary and transformed consciousness — five categories

Xuanzang's *Cheng Weishi Lun* organizes the eight vijñānas into a more nuanced hierarchical structure than Vasubandhu's original formulation. He distinguishes the ālayavijñāna (which he analyzes with unprecedented precision into its seed-bearing, maturing, and appropriating functions), the manas (which he characterizes as the source of the four fundamental kleśas: self-view, self-delusion, self-conceit, and self-love), and the remaining six consciousnesses as a group. He further distinguishes *pañca-vijñāna* (the five sensory consciousnesses operating simultaneously) from mano-vijñāna (which can operate in five modes: in conjunction with the senses, in dreaming, in meditative absorption, in unconscious states, and in the moment of dying). This five-mode analysis of mano-vijñāna is Xuanzang's original contribution and gives his system a more complete account of altered and transitional states of consciousness than any predecessor.
column5:
Thirty-six tattvas — consciousness contracting from absolute to atom

Abhinavagupta's account of levels of consciousness is the most architecturally elaborate in this table. The Kashmir Śaiva *tattva* system — thirty-six principles — describes the entire arc of consciousness contracting from its absolute form (Śiva as pure self-luminous awareness) through progressively denser and more limited forms down to the individual embodied ego and the material world. At the highest level: pure consciousness and pure bliss, without object or subject. Then the first contraction into the subject-object polarity. Then the cosmic principles of pure will, pure knowledge, pure action. Then *māyā* — the limiting principle that makes the absolute appear as finite. Then the five *kañcukas* — the sheaths that limit consciousness's omnipotence, omniscience, fullness, eternity, and omnipresence into the specific constraints of individual human experience. Then the individual mind, body, and the five elements. The thirty-six tattvas are not a cosmological curiosity; they are a precise map of where consciousness is in its journey of self-concealment and self-recognition.
column6:
Vegetative, animal, rational — faculties ascending to intellect

Ibn Sīnā inherits and transforms the Aristotelian faculty psychology into a sophisticated theory of cognitive levels. The vegetative soul (shared with plants) handles nutrition, growth, and reproduction — below the threshold of any awareness. The animal soul adds sensation and locomotion, introducing the first glimmers of conscious experience: the five external senses, the internal senses (common sense, imagination, estimative faculty, memory, fantasy), and the appetitive and locomotive powers. The rational soul — unique to humans — adds the faculty of intellect, which exists in four grades: the material intellect (pure potential), the habitual intellect (having acquired first principles), the actual intellect (actively thinking), and the acquired intellect (in contact with the Active Intellect, the cosmic principle of pure thought). The highest level of human consciousness is intellectual union with the Active Intellect — the moment when the individual mind temporarily participates in the universal mind that orders the cosmos.
column7:
Petites perceptions to apperception — an infinite gradient

Leibniz's theory of levels of consciousness is his most original contribution to the field and arguably the first modern formulation of the unconscious mind. Every monad perceives the entire universe, but most of this perception is *petites perceptions* — infinitesimally small, falling below the threshold of awareness, constituting what we would now call the unconscious. A threshold must be crossed — through the summation and reinforcement of minute perceptions — for a perception to become conscious. Above this threshold lies ordinary *perception* — the animals have this. Above that lies *apperception* — self-conscious awareness, the reflexive knowledge that one is conscious, which Leibniz reserves for rational souls. The significance of this hierarchy is that consciousness is not a sharp boundary but a continuous gradient: there is no point at which the unconscious ends and the conscious begins, only a zone of increasing clarity and self-reference. This places Leibniz as the direct ancestor of both psychoanalytic theory (the unconscious as structured like perception) and contemporary theories of consciousness as a spectrum.
column8:
Fringe to focus — the gradient of the stream

James's account of levels of consciousness is less taxonomic than the others in this table and more phenomenological: he is describing the internal structure of the stream rather than mapping its vertical hierarchy. Within any moment of consciousness there is a focal point — the object of explicit attention — surrounded by an ever-receding fringe of felt relations, tendencies, and half-articulated contents. Moving outward from the focus: the explicit object, then the felt relations holding that object in a context, then the penumbra of associations, then the bodily sense of orientation and emotion, then the dim background of habitual dispositions. James also distinguishes the ordinary waking stream from the extraordinary states accessible through religious experience, anesthesia, and drugs — arguing in *The Varieties of Religious Experience* that mystical states have noetic authority that ordinary consciousness cannot simply dismiss. Levels of consciousness for James are not a fixed hierarchy but a flowing field with variable topology.
column9:
Pre-reflective to reflective — the layered structure of the cogito

Husserl distinguishes carefully between *pre-reflective* and *reflective* consciousness. Pre-reflective consciousness is the primary mode: absorbed in its object, directed outward, not attending to itself. Reflective consciousness is a higher-order act in which consciousness turns back on itself and makes its own previous acts into objects of attention. This distinction matters enormously: what reflection reveals about consciousness is always a modified version of what was originally there — the act of attending to a perception is not the same as the original perception. Husserl also distinguishes levels in terms of temporal constitution: the living present contains a *primal impression* (the now-point), a *retention* (the just-past, still held in consciousness without being remembered), and a *protention* (the about-to-come, anticipated before it arrives). These three together constitute the specious present — the minimum structural unit of conscious experience, already internally complex.
column10:
Primary and higher-order — the biological divide

Edelman's most important contribution to the theory of levels is his distinction between *primary consciousness* and *higher-order consciousness*. Primary consciousness is the ability to construct a mental scene in the present — a moment-by-moment integration of perceptual, motor, and emotional information into a unified scene. Many animals have primary consciousness: it requires a nervous system capable of reentrant signaling between perceptual and value systems, but it does not require language or a concept of self. Higher-order consciousness is the ability to construct a model of the self, to situate that self in a remembered past and an anticipated future, and to use language to share and reflect on experience. It is the consciousness of being conscious. Only humans (and perhaps the great apes, to a limited degree) have higher-order consciousness. The divide between the two levels is not merely quantitative but qualitative: higher-order consciousness introduces a new dimension of temporal depth, self-knowledge, and symbolic representation that is absent from primary consciousness altogether.
column11:
Easy problems and the hard problem — a taxonomy of levels

Chalmers's taxonomy of levels is organized around his central distinction between the easy and hard problems. The 'easy problems' — explaining attention, memory, learning, emotional response, verbal report — concern the functional levels of consciousness, the cognitive mechanisms by which information is processed, integrated, and used to guide behavior. These are genuinely difficult scientific problems, but they are in principle solvable by the standard methods of cognitive science and neuroscience. The hard problem is categorically different: it asks why any of these functional processes are accompanied by subjective experience at all. Chalmers also distinguishes within phenomenal consciousness: there are differences in the *richness* of phenomenal experience (how much detail is present), the *unity* (why experience is unified into a single scene), the *transience* (why each moment gives way to the next), and the *ownership* (why experience is always experienced as mine). Each of these is a distinct question within the hard problem, and Chalmers's taxonomy makes clear that solving one does not solve the others.

column1:
Self

Witness, construct, or illusion?

Self synthesis

Three irreconcilable positions. No-self: Vasubandhu and Xuanzang — the self is manas perpetually misidentifying the ālayavijñāna; it is structurally generated, and liberation is its transformation into wisdom. Substantial self: Abhinavagupta (individual self is a contraction of Śiva's absolute aham), Ibn Sīnā (rational soul is a simple, immortal, self-knowing substance), Leibniz (monad as self-enclosed perspective unfolding from its internal law). Minimal or functional selfhood: Patanjali's featureless pure witness; James's stream-owner (the present thought appropriating its past); Husserl's transcendental ego (formal pole of all intentional acts); Edelman's biological self-model; Chalmers's phenomenal self (felt ownership — genuine but possibly constructed).
column2:
Puruṣa as witness — the self that sees but is never seen

Patanjali's account of the self is among the most radical in any tradition: the true self is *puruṣa* — pure consciousness that witnesses but is never itself an object of witnessing. The ordinary sense of self — the feeling of being a continuous person with memories, desires, and a name — belongs entirely to *prakṛti*, the material domain. It is the *ahaṃkāra* (ego-maker), a function of the material mind, that generates the illusion of a self by identifying the witness with the contents of experience. The witness itself has no qualities, no history, no individuality in the ordinary sense — it simply is. The profound implication is that what we ordinarily call 'myself' is entirely on the side of the object: the body, the senses, the mind, the intellect, the ego — all are objects witnessed by the puruṣa. Liberation is the dissolution of the false identification, leaving only the witness witnessing — but witnessing nothing, because there is no longer anything to bind it to the world of change.
column3:
Manas — the constructed self and its dissolution

Vasubandhu's account of the self is one of the most philosophically sophisticated in Buddhist philosophy. There is no permanent, unified self — the *ātman* that Brahmanical philosophy posits is a philosophical fiction. What exists in its place is the *manas* vijñāna: the seventh consciousness, which perpetually grasps the ālayavijñāna as if it were a permanent self. This grasping generates the four fundamental afflictions: self-view (the belief that there is a self), self-delusion (confusion about what that self is), self-conceit (the comparative ranking of that self), and self-love (attachment to that self). These four afflictions are the root of all suffering. The self is therefore not an illusion in the sense of being a simple mistake — it is a structurally generated misidentification, produced by a specific cognitive process (manas) acting on a specific object (ālayavijñāna). Liberation is the transformation of this misidentification — the conversion of manas from self-grasping to wisdom.
column4:
Ego-consciousness as structural illusion — the seventh vijñāna

Xuanzang's analysis of the self in the *Cheng Weishi Lun* is the most technically precise in the Yogācāra tradition. He identifies the self-sense as the function of the seventh vijñāna, manas, which operates continuously — in waking, dreaming, and deep sleep — taking the ālayavijñāna as its object and misidentifying it as a permanent, unitary self. What makes Xuanzang's account distinctive is his analysis of *why* this misidentification occurs: the ālayavijñāna has certain features — it is continuous, it underlies all experience, it is present across states — that make it a plausible candidate for selfhood. But it is not a self: it is an impermanent, causally constituted stream of consciousness. The ego-illusion is not random; it is a systematic misreading of genuine features of experience. Xuanzang also analyzes the conditions under which the seventh vijñāna is temporarily suspended — in deep meditative absorption and in the moments of transition at death — providing the most detailed account of ego-dissolution in the classical Buddhist corpus.
column5:
Aham — the self as the very pulse of absolute consciousness

Abhinavagupta's account of the self is the diametric opposite of the Buddhist no-self doctrine: the self is real, but it is not the limited ego — it is the absolute. The Sanskrit first-person pronoun *aham* is for Abhinavagupta not a pointer to an individual subject but the very vibration of absolute consciousness recognizing itself. The individual self is a contraction of this absolute self — real at its own level, but not ultimately distinct from Śiva. The ordinary ego, with its sense of boundaries, its anxiety, its need for self-preservation, is a real but limited manifestation of this deeper self-awareness. Liberation is not the dissolution of self into a featureless void; it is the recognition that the individual self was always already an expression of the absolute self. The self is not an illusion to be overcome but a contraction to be expanded — back into the fullness from which it came.
column6:
The rational soul — simple, immaterial, self-knowing

Ibn Sīnā's theory of the self follows from his Flying Man argument: the self is the rational soul, and the rational soul knows itself immediately, without mediation, without inference, without any bodily instrument. The soul is simple — without parts or extension — which is why it cannot be a property of the body or identified with any bodily state. Its simplicity guarantees its incorruptibility: a composite thing can disintegrate into its components; a simple thing has no components to disintegrate. The self is therefore naturally immortal — not by divine fiat but by metaphysical necessity. Ibn Sīnā also distinguishes the soul's self-knowledge from its knowledge of external objects: when the soul knows itself, there is no distinction between knower, known, and act of knowing — the self is transparent to itself in a way that no external object can be. This collapse of the subject-object distinction in self-knowledge anticipates Hegel and, through the Islamic tradition, Abhinavagupta's vimarśa.
column7:
The monad — a self-enclosed perspective with no windows

For Leibniz, the self is a monad — a simple, indivisible substance that perceives the entire universe from its unique perspective. The monad has no windows: nothing enters it from outside, and nothing exits it to the outside. Every perception, every thought, every feeling is unfolded from within the monad by its own internal law of development (*appetition*). The self is not constituted by its relations to others; it is constituted by its own internal life of perception and appetition, which mirrors the universe from a specific point of view. What makes human selves distinctive among monads is apperception — the capacity to reflect on their own perceptions and to know that they are perceiving. This self-reflexivity raises the human monad to the level of a rational soul, capable of moral responsibility, scientific knowledge, and the love of God. The self is thus for Leibniz both radically individual (each monad is unique, its law of development different from every other) and universally connected (each monad reflects all others, just more or less clearly).
column8:
The stream's owner — a self of pulse and tendency, not substance

James's account of the self is one of his most original and influential contributions. In Chapter X of the *Principles*, he distinguishes the *I* (the self as knower, the pure ego) from the *Me* (the self as known, the empirical self). The empirical self has three components: the material self (the body and its extensions, including clothes and possessions), the social self (the recognition one receives from others), and the spiritual self (the felt core of inner life, the sense of active thinking). The pure ego — the 'I' that does the knowing — is for James not a Cartesian substance or a Kantian transcendental unity, but simply the present thought, which appropriates the immediately preceding thought as its own past and will be appropriated by the immediately following thought as its past. The self is a river of appropriations, not a thing that does the appropriating. There is no soul-substance behind the stream; there is only the stream, doing what streams do.
column9:
Transcendental ego — the formal pole of all intentional acts

Husserl's account of the self undergoes a significant development across his career. In the *Logical Investigations* he is skeptical of the need to posit a pure ego distinct from the stream of experience. By the time of *Ideas I*, he has reversed course: every intentional act has two poles — the *noema* (the intended content) and the *noesis* (the intending act) — and the pure ego is the formal pole from which all acts emanate and to which all acts refer. The transcendental ego is not a substance, not a soul, not an empirical person — it is the minimal formal requirement for there to be intentional acts at all: the 'I' that intends, as distinct from what is intended. In the *Cartesian Meditations*, Husserl further analyzes how this formal ego constitutes its own temporal unity — how it identifies itself across time through the retention and protention structures of inner time-consciousness — and how it constitutes other egos as alter egos, similar but never fully accessible.
column10:
The embodied self — a biological process, not a homunculus

Edelman's account of the self is the most thoroughly deflationary in this table while stopping short of eliminativism. There is no homunculus — no central executive, no inner observer, no Cartesian theater in which experience is displayed to a self that watches. What we call the self is a biological process: specifically, the reentrant signaling between the dynamic core (which generates the conscious scene) and the value systems (which assign salience and urgency to elements of that scene) that occurs within the context of a body with a history. Higher-order consciousness introduces a model of the self — the ability to distinguish oneself from the world, to locate oneself in a past and future, to use the word 'I' and mean it — but this model is itself a product of neural processes, not their spectator. The self is real in the way that all biological processes are real: not reducible to physics but explainable in terms of it, not a fiction but not a substance either — a pattern maintained by the ongoing activity of the organism.
column11:
The phenomenal self — what it is like to be me

Chalmers's account of the self focuses on the phenomenal dimension: what is it like to be a self, to feel oneself as the continuing subject of experience? This phenomenal sense of self — the felt ownership of experiences, the felt continuity across time, the felt perspective from which the world is viewed — is for Chalmers a genuine phenomenal property, subject to all the difficulties of the hard problem. It cannot be explained functionally: even a philosophical zombie, by definition functionally identical to a human being, would process information from a first-person perspective without there being anything it is like to do so. The phenomenal self is not the same as personal identity in the psychological sense (the continuity of memories and character); it is the moment-by-moment felt presence of a subject. Chalmers also considers the possibility that the phenomenal self is a kind of construction — that the felt unity and continuity of selfhood may not accurately reflect the underlying structure of consciousness — but insists that even if constructed, it is still genuinely phenomenal: there is still something it is like to have this construction.

column1:
Time

Constituting or experiencing flow?

Time synthesis

Two fundamental questions divide the thinkers: Is temporal depth a formal structure of experience or a generated product? And is the felt flow of time a genuine phenomenal datum or a confused representation of something timeless? Husserl and James make temporal depth intrinsic to every moment — the living present always contains its just-past (retention) and about-to-come (protention); the specious present is a felt duration, not a knife-edge. Vasubandhu and Xuanzang generate continuity from rapid succession of discrete momentary arisings threaded by the ālayavijñāna. Patanjali and Abhinavagupta locate the deepest truth beyond time — the timeless witness, or the absolute's infinite simultaneity contracted into sequence. Edelman makes time a biological achievement; Chalmers makes the felt flow an irreducible phenomenal datum that physics cannot capture in its B-series.
column2:
Vṛtti and saṃskāra — mind as the repository of temporal traces

Patanjali's account of time in consciousness centers on the concept of *saṃskāra* — the impressions left in the mind by past experience. Every experience leaves a trace; traces generate tendencies; tendencies generate future experiences. The entire flow of conscious life is, for Patanjali, a cascade of these stored impressions activating in sequence, each moment of experience determined by the accumulated weight of all past experience. There is no neutral present moment: every present perception is colored by what has come before and oriented toward what is anticipated. The Yoga Sutras describe how even the experience of time itself — the sense of past, present, and future — is a mental construction, a *vṛtti* superimposed by the mind on the timeless witness. In deep samādhi, this temporal construction dissolves: the witness abides in a present that is not the present moment within time but the timeless now that underlies all temporal experience.
column3:
Momentariness — consciousness as a rapid series of discrete instants

Vasubandhu inherits and defends the Abhidharma doctrine of momentariness: consciousness does not flow continuously but arises and ceases in extremely brief moments, each distinct from the last. What appears to be a continuous stream of awareness is in fact an extremely rapid succession of discrete vijñāna-moments, each arising from the seeds of the previous moment and planting seeds for the next. The appearance of temporal continuity — the felt sense of a flowing present — is generated by the sheer speed of succession, like the frames of a film appearing as continuous motion. The ālayavijñāna provides the substrate for this succession: it is the storehouse that carries karmic seeds across the gaps between moments, maintaining the appearance of a continuous stream. Time, for Vasubandhu, is not a container in which consciousness exists but a structure *generated by* the sequential arising and ceasing of conscious moments.
column4:
Seed maturation and temporal continuity — the ālaya as time's loom

Xuanzang's account of time in consciousness centers on the mechanics of seed maturation in the ālayavijñāna. Seeds ripen according to their karmic weight and the conditions present in the stream of consciousness — some quickly, some over many lifetimes. The experienced present is the moment of a seed's maturation; the experienced past is the trace left by previous maturations; the anticipated future is the felt tendency of seeds not yet ripe. Time, in this account, is not an external framework but an internal rhythm of the consciousness-stream itself — the pulse of potentiality becoming actuality and actuality depositing new potentiality. Xuanzang gives particular attention to the problem of temporal continuity across radical discontinuities: how is the stream of consciousness maintained across dreamless sleep, across deep meditative states, and across the process of dying and rebirth? His answer is that the ālayavijñāna never fully ceases — it is the one vijñāna that persists through all apparent interruptions, serving as the loom on which the fabric of experienced time is woven.
column5:
Kāla and the vibrating present — time as consciousness pulsing

Abhinavagupta's account of time in consciousness is among the most philosophically original in this table. Time (*kāla*) is not external to consciousness; it is a contraction of consciousness's own unlimited self-presence. The absolute consciousness experiences everything simultaneously — past, present, and future are all equally present in its infinite self-awareness. When consciousness contracts into a finite form, this simultaneity is compressed into a sequence: what was all-at-once becomes before-and-after. The experienced present moment is not a knife-edge between past and future but a *spanda* — a vibration, a pulse of recognition, with its own internal depth. Abhinavagupta draws on the aesthetic theory of *rasa* to illuminate this: in the moment of deep aesthetic absorption, temporal sequence dissolves, and consciousness rests in an expanded present that resembles the timeless self-presence of the absolute. The aesthetics of time in Abhinavagupta is thus simultaneously a phenomenology and a metaphysics of consciousness.
column6:
Memory and anticipation — the soul's temporal reach

Ibn Sīnā's treatment of time in consciousness focuses on the internal senses that allow the soul to range beyond the present moment. The *memory faculty* (in Arabic psychology, divided into *dhikr* and *ḥifẓ*) stores the estimated meanings and emotional significance of past experiences, not just their sensory form. The *imagination* (or *khayāl*) retains sensory images after the original perception has ceased. The *estimative faculty* (*wahm*) grasps the practical significance of a perceived object — the sheep's immediate fear of the wolf — which transcends what the senses alone deliver. Together these faculties give the soul its temporal reach: the ability to act on the basis of past experience and anticipated future outcomes. For Ibn Sīnā, the rational soul's deepest temporal experience is its orientation toward eternal truths — the intellect in contact with the Active Intellect participates in a kind of timeless knowing that is the soul's highest form of temporal existence: touching eternity from within time.
column7:
Succession and simultaneity — time as the order of non-contemporaneous things

Leibniz's philosophy of time is one of the most original in the history of philosophy and has direct implications for his account of consciousness. Time is not an absolute container (Newton's view) nor a form of intuition (Kant's view) but the *order of non-contemporaneous things* — a relational structure generated by the causal succession of states within and between monads. For consciousness specifically, this means that temporal experience is constituted by the succession of perceptual states within the monad, each arising from the previous by internal law. The felt sense of temporal flow — the experience of past, present, and future — is a confused perception of this underlying causal order: we feel time flowing because our perceptions are causally ordered, but we perceive this order confusedly rather than clearly. The deepest implication is that the monad's entire history — past and future — is contained in its present state as a kind of infinite compressed complexity: God, perceiving the monad with infinite clarity, could read its entire history from any single moment.
column8:
The specious present — time as felt duration, not knife-edge

James's contribution to the phenomenology of time is the concept of the *specious present* — borrowed from E.R. Clay but given its classic formulation in the *Principles*. The experienced present is not a mathematical instant (a knife-edge with no duration) but a felt duration — a 'saddle-back' shape with a crest of vivid presentness and trailing edges of the just-past (still present) and the about-to-come (already anticipated). James estimates this specious present at roughly twelve seconds, though its duration varies with attention and emotional state. This means that the stream of consciousness never consists of discrete nows strung together; it consists of overlapping specious presents, each containing its own past and future within its felt span. Memory and anticipation are not separate acts added onto a bare now; they are structural features of every moment of experience, built into the very texture of the stream. Time-consciousness, for James, is a primary datum of experience, not a construction imposed on an originally timeless awareness.
column9:
Inner time-consciousness — retention, primal impression, protention

Husserl's analysis of inner time-consciousness — worked out in lectures from 1905 and published in 1928 — is widely regarded as the most rigorous phenomenological analysis of temporal experience ever produced. Every moment of consciousness has a triple structure: the *primal impression* (the living now-point, the absolute source), the *retention* (the immediate past, still held in the living present without being remembered — the tone just sounded, still resonating), and the *protention* (the imminent future, anticipated before it arrives — the next note of the melody, already expected). These three are not separate acts but moments within a single unified act: the living present is always already structured by its just-past and its about-to-come. This means that consciousness never lives in a dimensionless now: it always inhabits a thick present with temporal depth. Husserl further analyzes how retentions of retentions constitute memory, how protentions of protentions constitute anticipation, and how the entire structure is unified by what he calls the *absolute flux* of time-consciousness — which he admits is the most difficult and perhaps unanswerable problem in his phenomenology.
column10:
The remembered present — primary consciousness as temporal integration

Edelman's name for his theory of consciousness — *the remembered present* — is itself a statement about time. Primary consciousness is the construction of a mental scene in the present moment, but this construction is not timeless: it requires the integration of present perceptual input with value-laden memory systems that record the organism's past history of interaction with the world. The 'present' of consciousness is always already colored by the remembered past — not through explicit recollection but through the value systems that have been shaped by previous experience and now automatically weight and color the incoming perceptual stream. This is why an animal with primary consciousness does not merely perceive; it perceives *in terms of* what has mattered before. Higher-order consciousness adds an explicit temporal dimension: the ability to locate a remembered past and an anticipated future, to know that one existed before this moment and will exist after it. Time-consciousness, for Edelman, is thus a biological achievement rather than a formal structure: it is earned by evolutionary history and neural development.
column11:
Temporal experience as phenomenal — the felt flow irreducible to physics

Chalmers's approach to time in consciousness is shaped by his general framework: the phenomenal experience of time — the felt flow from past through present to future, the sense of duration, the vivid nowness of the present moment — is a genuine phenomenal property and therefore subject to the hard problem. Physics, as Chalmers notes following a long tradition from McTaggart onward, describes time in terms of an objective B-series (earlier than, later than, simultaneous with) but has no place for the phenomenal A-series: the felt distinction between past (gone), present (vividly now), and future (not yet). The experienced flow of time — the sense that the present is moving, that the future is becoming the present and the present is becoming the past — is not captured by any physical description of temporal relations. It is a phenomenal datum, irreducible to function or structure, which any complete theory of consciousness must account for. Chalmers thus sees the phenomenology of time as one of the deepest challenges for physicalist theories of mind.

column1:
Body

Embodied or independent?

Body synthesis

The range runs from Patanjali's absolute dualism (consciousness never touches the body; liberation is recognizing they were always distinct) to Abhinavagupta's continuum (body is consciousness at its densest — flesh is frozen light — making it a vehicle of liberation, not an obstacle). Vasubandhu and Xuanzang hold that the body is a projection of the ālaya. Ibn Sīnā's soul uses the body as an instrument; Leibniz coordinates soul and body through pre-established harmony with no real causal interaction. James makes consciousness deeply somatic (we are afraid because we tremble); Husserl makes the lived body the zero-point of all spatial orientation; Edelman makes consciousness impossible without the full biological organism and its evolutionary history of values. Chalmers alone remains open to disembodied phenomenal consciousness.
column2:
Prakṛti as the field — consciousness witnesses but never touches matter

Patanjali's account of the body-consciousness relation is one of the most radical in this table: consciousness and matter are absolutely distinct. Puruṣa (consciousness) and prakṛti (matter-energy) are two eternal, irreducible principles. Their apparent association is the root of all suffering and all bondage. The body, the nervous system, even the most refined states of the mind — all belong to prakṛti. Consciousness does not interact with the body; it merely witnesses it. The appearance of interaction — the sense that I decide to move my arm and my arm moves — is a product of their apparent association, not evidence of genuine causal connection. This absolute dualism has a liberating implication: since consciousness never actually touches matter, there is nothing matter can do to consciousness. Liberation is not an achievement but a recognition — the recognition that consciousness was always already free, always already uninvolved, always already a pure witness. The body and its sufferings are real at the level of prakṛti; they have no purchase on puruṣa whatsoever.
column3:
Consciousness projects the body — organic form as mental representation

Vasubandhu's account reverses the common-sense picture entirely: the body is not the container or substrate of consciousness but a product of consciousness. What appears as a body — its sensations, its boundaries, its biological processes — is a representation arising from the ālayavijñāna, no more independently real than the objects of a dream. The felt sense of embodiment — the proprioceptive awareness of having a body located in space, the visceral sense of hunger and pain — is vijñāna arising from the maturation of seeds stored in the ālaya, not a report from an independently existing physical organism. This is not to say that the body is unimportant: the specific form of the body, with its specific sensory capacities and its specific vulnerabilities, shapes the structure of the consciousness-stream that projects it. But causality runs from consciousness to body, not from body to consciousness.
column4:
The body as the support-basis of consciousness — āśraya and āśrita

Xuanzang introduces a technical distinction that nuances the Yogācāra body-consciousness relation: the body is the *āśraya* (support-basis) of the sensory vijñānas, not their cause. The eye-consciousness does not arise because of the eye in a straightforward causal sense; the eye is a necessary supporting condition — without a functioning visual organ, visual consciousness cannot arise — but it does not produce visual consciousness the way a cause produces its effect. Visual consciousness arises from the seeds in the ālayavijñāna; the eye is the condition that determines the specific form that arising takes. This subtle distinction allows Xuanzang to maintain that consciousness is ultimately self-arising (from seeds) while accounting for the obvious fact that damage to the sense organs affects the quality of sensory experience. The body is thus neither the cause of consciousness (as materialism holds) nor irrelevant to it (as some idealisms imply) but the specific conditioned form through which consciousness manifests in a given life.
column5:
The body as contracted consciousness — flesh as frozen light

Abhinavagupta's account of the body-consciousness relation is the most metaphysically daring in this table: the body is not other than consciousness. Matter is consciousness contracted to its densest, most limited form — what Abhinavagupta calls the *pṛthivī tattva* (earth principle) at the base of the thirty-six tattva hierarchy. The flesh is, in a precise sense, frozen light: the same absolute consciousness that shines as pure self-awareness in its highest form has contracted so completely that it appears as inert, extended, impenetrable matter. This means there is no hard boundary between mind and body: they exist on a single continuum of consciousness condensing. The practical implication is that the body can be used as a vehicle of liberation — not escaped from, as in the Sāṃkhya-Yoga dualism, but transformed from within, through tantric practice that awakens the dormant consciousness sleeping in the flesh. The body is sacred not despite being matter but because matter is consciousness in its deepest disguise.
column6:
The soul uses the body — instrument without identification

Ibn Sīnā's account of the body-soul relation is fundamentally instrumentalist: the rational soul uses the body as an instrument but is not identical with it, not a product of it, and not essentially dependent on it for its existence. The soul uses the body's sense organs to receive information about the material world, and it uses the body's motor systems to act in that world. But the soul itself — in its highest rational function — acts independently of the body: intellectual cognition of universal concepts requires no bodily organ. This claim is Ibn Sīnā's most significant philosophical innovation: Aristotle had tied the intellect to the body more closely; Ibn Sīnā cuts that tie. The Flying Man argument is precisely designed to show that the soul's self-knowledge — its most intimate cognitive act — requires no body at all. Death, therefore, is the separation of the soul from its instrument, not the destruction of the soul. What the soul loses at death is its access to sensory information; what it retains is everything it truly is.
column7:
Body as the lowest monad — matter as perception below threshold

Leibniz's account of the body-consciousness relation follows directly from his monadology: there is no matter that is not, at some level, perception. Every material particle is a monad with some degree of perception, however dim and unconscious — what he calls *bare monads* or *sleeping monads*. The human body is an aggregate of monads, each perceiving its neighborhood from its own perspective, coordinated by the dominant monad of the soul. There is no genuine interaction between the soul and the body — Leibniz's *pre-established harmony* holds that God has coordinated soul and body to correspond perfectly without any causal influence passing between them. What appears as the body causing mental states, or mental states causing bodily movements, is a perfect synchrony established at creation, not a real causal relation. The practical consequence is that Leibniz avoids both Cartesian interactionism (which requires an impossible mechanism) and epiphenomenalism (which makes mind causally inert): soul and body are parallel systems, perfectly coordinated, neither causing the other.
column8:
The body's own thought — consciousness rooted in habit and flesh

James's treatment of the body-consciousness relation is one of his most influential contributions — particularly his account of habit and emotion. In *The Principles of Psychology*, he argues that the body is not merely the instrument of consciousness but its very substrate: the brain is the organ of thought, and every mental state corresponds to a specific pattern of neural activity. More radically, his theory of emotion reverses the common-sense picture: we do not tremble because we are afraid; we are afraid because we tremble. The bodily state — the racing heart, the tight chest, the trembling limbs — is not the expression of an antecedently existing emotion; it *is* the emotion, perceived from within. Consciousness is thus deeply somatic: it arises from and within the body's ongoing processes, not above them. This proto-phenomenological insight anticipates Merleau-Ponty and, through James, connects directly to Edelman's biological account of consciousness as a whole-organism process.
column9:
Kinaesthetic body-schema — consciousness constitutes its own flesh

Husserl's account of the body-consciousness relation is built around the distinction between the body as *Körper* (objective, measurable, third-person body) and the body as *Leib* (lived body, the body as experienced from within). The lived body is not an object among objects in the world; it is the zero-point of orientation from which all other objects are experienced — the here from which everything else is there. Husserl's analysis of the lived body focuses on its double nature: the hand can touch and be touched simultaneously, occupying both the subject and object positions at once. This double sensation is, for Husserl, the founding structure of all spatial constitution: consciousness knows the world as extended and spatialized because it inhabits an extended, spatialized body that it both is and has. The body is therefore not the prison of consciousness (Platonic) nor its product (Yogācāra) nor merely its instrument (Ibn Sīnā) but its very medium of worldly engagement.
column10:
Embodied consciousness — the thalamocortical system within the organism

Edelman's account of the body-consciousness relation is the most fully biological in this table: consciousness does not exist in a brain in a vat. It exists in a brain that is part of a body — a body with a specific evolutionary history, a specific developmental trajectory, a specific set of value systems shaped by the needs of an organism that must eat, avoid predators, find mates, and navigate a physical world. The dynamic core that generates consciousness is not isolated within the cortex; it is continuously informed by signals from the body's interior (homeostatic states, hormonal levels, visceral sensations) and from the body's surface (proprioception, touch, pain). Damasio's concept of the somatic marker — the role of body-state signals in guiding rational decision-making — is closely related to Edelman's account, and both are making the same fundamental point: consciousness is not a disembodied information processor but an organ of a living organism, shaped by and serving the organism's biological needs.
column11:
Phenomenal properties of the body — felt embodiment as hard problem instance

Chalmers's treatment of body-consciousness takes the form of analyzing bodily phenomenology as a domain of hard problem instances. The felt qualities of the body — the specific painfulness of a headache, the felt warmth of a fever, the proprioceptive sense of one's limbs' position in space — are all genuine phenomenal properties. They are not functional states: even a complete description of nociceptive signaling pathways would leave open why there is *something it is like* to be in pain rather than merely a system responding to tissue damage. Chalmers also engages with the philosophical question of whether phenomenal consciousness is always embodied — whether the sense of inhabiting a body is a necessary feature of any possible experience, or whether disembodied consciousness is conceivable.
