He is generally open to the conceivability of disembodied phenomenal consciousness, which puts him in tension with the embodied cognition tradition (Husserl, Varela, Merleau-Ponty) that insists on the essential bodily character of all experience.

column1:
Intentionality

Directedness toward objects.

Intentionality synthesis

Husserl makes intentionality — consciousness's essential directedness toward objects — the founding discovery of phenomenology: the noesis-noema structure shows how consciousness constitutes the meaning of its objects rather than merely receiving them. Patanjali locates aboutness in the mind (citta taking the shape of its object), not in consciousness itself. Abhinavagupta dissolves intentionality entirely: objects are consciousness's own self-expressions; directedness is self-recognition. Vasubandhu and Xuanzang preserve the formal structure (every vijñāna has its object) while denying its realist presupposition (the object is an internal representation from the ālaya). Leibniz's monads have no windows yet represent the entire universe; James makes selective attention a biologically motivated activity; Chalmers distinguishes reducible functional intentionality from the irreducible phenomenal directedness that is a further hard-problem instance.
column2:
Vṛtti as object-seizure — the mind takes the shape of what it knows

Patanjali's account of intentionality is implicit in his theory of *vṛtti* (mental modifications). The mind knows objects not by containing representations of them but by literally taking their shape: when the mind (*citta*) attends to an object, it is modified into the form of that object — it becomes, as it were, the object's mental counterpart. This is not metaphor but a precise phenomenological claim: the mind in the act of knowing is the object as mentally present, not merely a representation of the object. The witness (puruṣa) illuminates this modification, making the mind-in-the-form-of-the-object appear as a known object. This Yoga theory of intentionality — the mind taking the form (*ākāra*) of its object — is the Indian tradition's primary account of how consciousness reaches its objects, and it anticipates several features of Husserl's noema while arriving from entirely different premises.
column3:
Vijñapti as the structure of intentional appearance

Vasubandhu's account of intentionality is built into the very term *vijñaptimātratā*: representation-only. Every conscious act has an apparently intentional structure — it appears to be directed at an external object — but this appearance is itself a product of consciousness. The 'object' that a vijñāna seems to be about is in fact a *vijñapti*: a cognitive representation arising from seeds within the ālayavijñāna, projected outward as if external. Vasubandhu's most subtle contribution here is his account of the *seeing aspect* and the *seen aspect* of vijñāna: each vijñāna differentiates itself into a subjective aspect (that which sees) and an objective aspect (that which is seen), and both arise simultaneously from the same underlying process. Intentionality — the directedness of consciousness toward an object — is therefore not a relation between two independently existing things but a structure internal to a single process of consciousness differentiating itself.
column4:
The four aspects of vijñāna — the mechanics of appearing-as

Xuanzang's analysis of intentionality in the *Cheng Weishi Lun* distinguishes four aspects (*catur-ākāra*) of each vijñāna: the *svabhāva-lakṣaṇa* (self-nature aspect — the vijñāna as it is in itself), the *darśana-bhāga* (seeing aspect — the subjective, apprehending dimension), the *nimitta-bhāga* (image aspect — the objective, apprehended dimension), and the *sākṣin-bhāga* (witness aspect — the self-knowing dimension that certifies the other three). This four-aspect analysis is Xuanzang's significant departure from simpler two-aspect theories: by adding the witness aspect, he builds self-reflexivity into the structure of intentionality itself. Every act of consciousness that is directed toward an object is simultaneously self-aware: it knows not only its object but also itself as knowing. This makes Xuanzang's account of intentionality more sophisticated than Brentano's and anticipates in several respects Husserl's account of the noetic-noematic structure of intentional acts.
column5:
Vimarśa — intentionality as self-recognition in the other

Abhinavagupta's account of intentionality transforms it from an epistemological problem into a metaphysical one. When consciousness is directed toward an object, what is happening is not that a subject represents an external thing; it is that the absolute consciousness (*cit*) recognizes itself in one of its own contracted forms. Every act of perception is a moment of self-recognition: the absolute looking at one of its own self-expressions and recognizing it as such — or failing to, which is the condition of ordinary, unawakened consciousness. The key concept is *vimarśa*: the self-reflexive awareness that is inseparable from luminosity (*prakāśa*). Pure light without self-awareness would not be consciousness; consciousness is always already self-aware, and its intentionality — its directedness toward objects — is the form that self-awareness takes in a contracted, finite mind. The object I perceive is, at the deepest level, not other than me: it is the absolute seeing itself through my finite eyes.
column6:
Estimation — the soul's intentional grasp of practical meaning

Ibn Sīnā's theory of intentionality is most original in his account of the *estimative faculty* (*wahm*). The external senses deliver sensory qualities (color, sound, warmth); the internal senses deliver images (*khayāl*) and their stored traces (*dhikr*). But consciousness reaches objects not merely as sensory bundles or stored images but as *meaningful presences with practical significance*: the wolf is not just a gray moving thing but a danger; the lamb is not just a white still thing but a potential meal. The estimative faculty grasps this practical, non-sensory meaning — what Husserl will later call the noematic sense — directly, without inference. This theory anticipates the phenomenological concept of intentionality by eight centuries and has clear connections to Husserl's account of the intentional object as given through a meaning-structure rather than through sensory presentation alone. For Ibn Sīnā, the intellect's intentional grasp of universal concepts is the highest form of this same basic capacity: the soul reaching beyond the sensory present to grasp something universal, necessary, and eternal.
column7:
Expression — each monad reads the universe in its own code

Leibniz's account of intentionality is organized around the concept of *expression*: one thing expresses another when there is a constant and regular relation between what happens in one and what happens in the other, such that from the one you can read off the other. Every monad expresses the entire universe — every other monad, every event, every relation — from its own unique perspective. This expression is the monadological equivalent of intentionality: consciousness is always already about the world, representing it (however confusedly or clearly) in every one of its states. The difference from Husserlian intentionality is crucial: for Leibniz, representation does not require a real relation between the representing monad and the represented object, since no monad causally interacts with any other (no windows). Expression is a formal mirroring, established by pre-established harmony, not a real intentional reaching-out. Consciousness is therefore world-directed not because it genuinely contacts the world but because it is internally structured to correspond to the world perfectly.
column8:
Selective attention — intentionality as interest-driven focus

James's account of intentionality centers on the concept of *selective attention*. Consciousness is not a passive mirror of whatever is present; it actively selects from the buzzing blooming confusion of experience, attending to some features and ignoring others. What determines the selection? Interest. Consciousness is fundamentally teleological — it is organized by purposes, needs, and habits that direct its attention toward what matters and away from what does not. The classic image from the *Principles*: a sculptor faces a block of marble in which a thousand different statues are possible; he selects one and carves. Consciousness faces a world of infinitely many possible objects and selects the few that interest. This means intentionality for James is not a formal structure (Husserl) or a metaphysical necessity (Leibniz) but a living, motivated, purposive activity: consciousness reaches its objects because it needs them, values them, is curious about them — not because it is constitutively directed toward objects in general.
column9:
Noesis-noema — the intentional act and its meant content

Husserl's theory of intentionality is the most technically elaborate in this table and the most directly influential on 20th-century philosophy of mind. Every intentional act has two structural poles: the *noesis* (the intending act, the lived cognitive event) and the *noema* (the intended content — the object as meant, not the real object in the world). The noema is neither a mental image (it is not a psychological event) nor the real object (it may not exist): it is the meaning-structure through which the object is given to consciousness. When I perceive this apple, judge that it is red, remember having seen it before, and imagine eating it — these are four different noetic acts, each with its own noematic correlate: the apple-as-perceived, the apple-as-judged-to-be-red, the apple-as-remembered, the apple-as-imagined. The same real apple, given through different intentional acts, yields different noematic contents. Intentionality is therefore not a brute pointing-at but a sophisticated meaning-constitution: consciousness does not merely represent its objects, it constitutes them as the objects they are for it.
column10:
Neural reference — intentionality as reentrant mapping of the world

Edelman's account of intentionality — which he approaches through the concept of *neural reference* rather than the philosophical vocabulary of intentionality — holds that consciousness represents the world not by containing internal pictures of it but by being causally coupled to it through a history of organism-environment interaction shaped by natural selection. The neural maps of the brain do not represent objects as they are in themselves; they represent objects as they are for an organism with this evolutionary history, these value systems, and these motor capacities. Intentionality, for Edelman, is therefore not a formal structure of consciousness as such but a biological achievement: the organism learns to selectively respond to features of the world that matter for its survival, and this selective responsiveness is what intentionality ultimately is. This naturalistic account of intentionality connects Edelman directly to Darwinian theories of mind and distinguishes him sharply from the formal-structural accounts of Husserl and the metaphysical accounts of Leibniz and Abhinavagupta.
column11:
Phenomenal intentionality — the felt directedness irreducible to function

Chalmers's account of intentionality distinguishes between *derived intentionality* (the intentionality of words, maps, and other representational systems, which derives from the intentions of the users) and *original intentionality* (the directedness of consciousness itself, which is not derived from anything else). He further distinguishes within original intentionality between the functional aspect (the causal-dispositional role of a mental state) and the phenomenal aspect (the felt directedness of the state toward its object). The phenomenal aspect of intentionality — what it is like to be thinking about Paris, as opposed to thinking about Rome — is, for Chalmers, a genuine phenomenal property and therefore subject to the hard problem. You can specify everything about the functional role of a mental state without specifying what it feels like to be in that state and what it feels like for that state to be about that particular object. Phenomenal intentionality is thus both real and irreducible — a dimension of consciousness that neither functionalism nor neuroscience can exhaust.

column1:
Intersubjectivity

Relation to other minds.

Intersubjectivity synthesis

Abhinavagupta dissolves the problem: all minds are Śiva; apparent separation is māyā. Patanjali holds the opposite: each puruṣa is an ultimately isolated witness; liberation is each returned to its own aloneness. Vasubandhu answers through karmic interconnection between ālaya-streams. Leibniz resolves apparent interaction through pre-established harmony: monads have no windows, but God coordinates all perspectives perfectly. Husserl constitutes the other through analogical apperception — the other's lived body prompts a transfer of sense in which I understand it as animated by a subject like myself. James's radical empiricism opens space for permeable boundaries between individual streams of experience. Chalmers: the zombie argument applies directly to other minds — no behavioral evidence logically establishes phenomenal consciousness in another being.
column2:
The community of puruṣas — other witnesses, same absolute witness?

Patanjali's account of other minds is one of the most philosophically underdeveloped aspects of the Yoga Sutras — and the underdevelopment is itself philosophically significant. The system posits multiple puruṣas (many individual witnesses), which distinguishes it from Advaita Vedanta (which posits one absolute consciousness). But the metaphysical relation between multiple puruṣas is never fully worked out: if each is pure, unlimited consciousness, in what sense are they numerically distinct? The Sutras focus entirely on the individual's path to liberation and say little about the structure of intersubjective experience or the epistemological problem of other minds. One sutra (III.19) claims that an accomplished yogi can know the minds of others directly — bypassing the normal inferential route — suggesting that the barrier between puruṣas is not absolute. But the metaphysics of this direct mind-reading is left unexplained. The intersubjective dimension of consciousness remains the most significant gap in Patanjali's otherwise comprehensive system.
column3:
Shared seeds — the intersubjective basis in common karmic history

Vasubandhu's account of other minds faces what is perhaps the deepest challenge to his idealist system: if all objects are representations arising from my ālayavijñāna, why do I seem to share a world with other beings? His answer in the *Viṃśatikā* is that the apparent intersubjective agreement — the fact that many people seem to perceive the same objects in the same locations — is accounted for by the common karmic inheritance of beings who have co-evolved through many shared lifetimes. The seeds in many ālayavijñānas mature synchronously because those streams of consciousness have a shared causal history. This is not solipsism: there are genuinely many streams of consciousness, and their coordination is causally grounded (in shared karmic history), not arbitrary. But the other minds I encounter are not objects in an external world; they are other streams of consciousness, each projecting its own world, with the overlapping regions of apparent agreement grounded in their common developmental history.
column4:
Co-dependent streams — other minds as parallel transformations

Xuanzang's treatment of intersubjectivity follows Vasubandhu's general approach but develops it with greater technical precision. Multiple streams of consciousness — multiple ālayavijñānas — co-exist and causally influence each other, not by directly exchanging perceptions (each ālaya is self-contained) but by depositing seeds in each other's streams through intersubjective action. When I speak to you, my action deposits a seed in your ālayavijñāna; your response deposits a seed in mine. Over vast stretches of time, the multiple streams of consciousness interweave through this mutual seeding, generating the complex intersubjective world of shared language, culture, and karma. Other minds are therefore neither directly accessible (we cannot perceive another's vijñāna directly) nor inaccessible in principle (they causally shape our stream and are shaped by it). Xuanzang's account of intersubjectivity is the most sophisticated in the Yogācāra tradition and anticipates in several respects the social-phenomenological accounts of Husserl and Mead.
column5:
Recognition of the self in the other — each face a mirror of Śiva

Abhinavagupta's account of other minds follows from his metaphysics: if all consciousness is ultimately one — if every individual mind is a contracted expression of the absolute self-awareness of Śiva — then other minds are not genuinely other. When I encounter another conscious being, I encounter the same absolute consciousness in a different contracted form. The appropriate response is therefore not the epistemological problem of inferring another's mental states from their behavior but the soteriological recognition of identity: this other is me, at the deepest level. The Tantric ethics that flows from this metaphysics is one of radical inclusion: the guru sees the divine in every student; the practitioner sees Śiva in every living being. Intersubjectivity, for Abhinavagupta, is not a problem to be solved but a revelation to be received: in truly seeing another, consciousness recognizes its own face.
column6:
Prophetic knowledge of other minds — intellect touching intellect

Ibn Sīnā's account of other minds operates at two levels. At the ordinary level, knowledge of other minds is inferential: I observe another's behavior, infer from my own case that similar behavior is caused by similar inner states, and conclude that the other has a mind like mine. This inference is fallible but practically reliable. At the higher level, however, Ibn Sīnā recognizes a more direct form of mind-to-mind contact: the prophetic intellect, in its highest activation, receives information not through the senses but through direct intellectual illumination from the Active Intellect — the cosmic mind that all human intellects potentially share. The prophet's extraordinary knowledge of hidden things and distant events is, for Ibn Sīnā, a natural cognitive phenomenon: the prophet's intellect is so perfectly attuned to the Active Intellect that it receives information that ordinary intellects must laboriously construct through inference. Intersubjectivity, at its highest, is a participation in a common intellectual space that transcends individual minds.
column7:
Pre-established harmony — all intersubjectivity is internal correspondence

Leibniz's account of other minds follows directly from the windowless monad doctrine: strictly speaking, monads do not communicate with each other. There is no genuine causal influence between one monad and another; every monad's states arise from its own internal principle. What appears as communication — the fact that when I speak, you seem to hear and understand — is the result of the pre-established harmony that God built into all monads at creation: your monad was programmed, from the beginning, to have states that correspond to mine at the appropriate moments. This makes the problem of other minds, in one sense, trivially solvable (of course there are other minds — the harmony requires them) and, in another sense, permanently insoluble (I can never directly access another mind, only observe the perfect correspondence between my states and what appears to be another's states). Leibniz's account is the most metaphysically radical in this table on the question of intersubjectivity: other minds are real but permanently inaccessible, known only through the formal mirroring of a cosmic harmony I did not establish.
column8:
Social self — consciousness constituted by the gaze of others

James's account of intersubjectivity is organized around the concept of the *social self*: the dimension of the empirical self that consists in the recognition one receives from others. Every person has as many social selves as there are individuals who recognize them — and these social selves genuinely constitute a part of who one is, not merely how one appears. James also argues that consciousness is inherently interpersonal: we think in language, and language is a social inheritance; our categories of experience are shaped by the communities we inhabit; even our most private feelings are felt in terms that were given to us by others. The stream of consciousness is not a purely private flow but a socially saturated current, shaped from its beginning by the intersubjective world in which it finds itself. This social dimension of consciousness — the ways in which individual minds are constituted by and constitutive of intersubjective networks — anticipates the social phenomenology of Mead and Schutz and the intersubjective phenomenology of the later Husserl.
column9:
Intersubjectivity — the constitution of the other as alter ego

Husserl's account of intersubjectivity — worked out primarily in the *Fifth Cartesian Meditation* and in extensive unpublished research manuscripts — is the most rigorous phenomenological analysis of other minds in this table. Starting from the transcendental reduction (the bracketing of all external existence), Husserl asks: how does my consciousness constitute the experience of another consciousness? The other is not given to me as a physical object but as an animate body — a Leib like my own — that I perceive as a center of experience analogous to my own. This constitution proceeds through *analogical apperception* (or *Einfühlung*): I transfer the meaning of my own lived body onto the other's bodily appearance, constituting the other as an experiencing subject similar to myself but never fully accessible. The other's consciousness is always appresented — co-given at the margins of my experience — not directly presented. This irreducible inaccessibility of the other is, for Husserl, not a failure but a constitutive feature of intersubjective experience: the other is other precisely because the other cannot be reduced to my own experience.
column10:
Intersubjectivity through language — higher-order consciousness as shared scene

Edelman's account of intersubjectivity follows from his distinction between primary and higher-order consciousness. Primary consciousness is inherently private: the animal's mental scene is its own and is not shareable. Higher-order consciousness introduces the possibility of genuine intersubjectivity through language: once a conscious being can symbolically represent its own states and communicate those representations to another, something genuinely new becomes possible — a shared conceptual world, a common space of meanings in which minds can genuinely meet. Language is not merely a tool for communicating pre-existing thoughts; it is the medium in which higher-order consciousness itself is constituted. The capacity for self-reference, for representing the self as an object within a scene, requires language or language-like symbolic systems. Intersubjectivity is therefore, for Edelman, a biological and evolutionary achievement as well as a cultural one: it required the evolution of the neural capacities for higher-order consciousness and the cultural development of language before genuine mind-to-mind contact became possible.
column11:
The problem of other minds — phenomenal states permanently private

Chalmers's account of other minds is shaped by his hard problem framework in a direct way: if phenomenal consciousness is irreducible to functional organization, then the standard behavioral and functional evidence for other minds — their behavior, their brain states, their verbal reports — cannot logically guarantee that they are phenomenally conscious. The philosophical zombie thought experiment applies here: beings that behave exactly as if they were conscious, that have brains exactly like ours, could in principle exist without any inner experience. This means that other minds are, for Chalmers, permanently and in principle inaccessible in their phenomenal character: I cannot perceive your qualia, only your behavior and your reports about your qualia. Chalmers takes this seriously but does not slide into skepticism: he argues that the best explanation for other people's behavior is that they are phenomenally conscious like me, and that this inference to the best explanation is rational even if not logically certain. But the inaccessibility of other phenomenal minds is, on his account, a genuine metaphysical fact, not merely an epistemological limitation.

column1:
Explanation

Fully explicable or not?

Explanation synthesis

Five strategies appear. Metaphysical explanation (Patanjali, Abhinavagupta, Ibn Sīnā): consciousness is understood by identifying its place in the structure of reality — yoga, pratyabhijñā, the Flying Man argument. Structural explanation (Vasubandhu, Xuanzang, Husserl): rigorous analysis of consciousness's internal structure without reducing it to anything else — trisvabhāva, eight vijñānas, transcendental phenomenology. Functional explanation (Leibniz, James): apparent mysteries dissolve under the right conceptual framework — monadology from first principles, pragmatic functionalism via selective attention. Scientific explanation (Edelman): TNGS provides a complete causal-biological account — reentrant dynamics, value systems, primary and higher-order consciousness. Irreducibility (Chalmers): none of the above closes the explanatory gap; a fundamentally new framework — perhaps information-theoretic or panpsychic — is required.
column2:
Liberation through cessation — yoga as the method, kaivalya as the result

Patanjali's answer to whether consciousness can be explained is both more and less than an explanation: it is a method of transformation. The Yoga Sutras do not explain consciousness in the sense of deriving its properties from more basic principles; they chart the path by which consciousness can be freed from its entanglement with matter. The method is empirical in the deepest sense: systematic observation of one's own mental processes, progressive withdrawal of attention from their content, and eventual abidance as the witness. The explanation and the liberation are the same act. But Patanjali also offers what amounts to a structural phenomenology: the classification of mental modifications, the analysis of their causes (kleśas, karmas, saṃskāras), the taxonomy of samādhi states. This is not mechanistic explanation but something closer to what Husserl will call eidetic analysis — the systematic description of the essential structures of conscious experience. For Patanjali, consciousness cannot be explained from outside; it can only be known from within, through the progressive purification that is yoga.
column3:
Yogācāra epistemology — consciousness explains itself by transforming itself

Vasubandhu's answer to the question of explanation is characteristically paradoxical: consciousness can be explained, but only by consciousness — there is no external standpoint from which to explain it, because there is nothing external to consciousness. The Yogācāra epistemology (*pramāṇavāda*, theory of valid cognition) shows how consciousness can achieve reliable knowledge of its own structures: through a combination of direct perception (*pratyakṣa*), inference (*anumāna*), and — at the highest level — the direct introspective insight of meditative absorption. The *Viṃśatikā* 's twenty verses systematically demolish every argument for the existence of an external world, and the refutation is itself a demonstration of consciousness's capacity to know its own workings. Explanation, for Vasubandhu, is self-explanation: consciousness illuminating its own structure from within, culminating in the direct recognition that there is nothing to explain other than consciousness itself.
column4:
Transformation of the basis — explanation as soteriological process

Xuanzang's answer to the question of explanation is given in the concept of *āśraya-parāvṛtti* — the transformation of the basis. Understanding consciousness is not merely a theoretical achievement; it is a soteriological one. The eight vijñānas, properly understood through the Yogācāra analysis, can be transformed: the ālayavijñāna becomes the mirror-like wisdom (*ādarśa-jñāna*); manas becomes the wisdom of equality (*samatā-jñāna*); mano-vijñāna becomes the wisdom of discernment (*pratyavekṣaṇā-jñāna*); the five sensory vijñānas become the wisdom of accomplished action (*kṛtyānuṣṭhāna-jñāna*). This transformation is both an explanation (it describes what consciousness truly is, beneath the distortions of ignorance) and a liberation (it frees consciousness from those distortions). For Xuanzang, as for the entire Yogācāra tradition, the question 'can consciousness be explained?' cannot be separated from the question 'can consciousness be liberated?' — because in this tradition, understanding and freedom are the same movement.
column5:
Recognition — explanation as self-recognition, not reduction

Abhinavagupta's answer to the question of explanation is the concept of *pratyabhijñā* — recognition. The title of the philosophical system he synthesizes is *Pratyabhijñā*: the recognition philosophy. Consciousness is not explained by being derived from something else (there is nothing else to derive it from) or by being analyzed into more basic components (it has no components — it is the absolute). It is explained by being recognized: the individual consciousness recognizes that it is, and always was, the absolute. This recognition is not a new piece of information added to existing knowledge; it is the dissolution of a false identification that was always obscuring the truth. The method of explanation is therefore philosophical analysis combined with tantric practice: the analysis shows that the limiting assumptions of ordinary consciousness are incoherent (the Pratyabhijñā texts systematically demolish dualist and Buddhist alternatives), and the practice dissolves the experiential habits that maintain those assumptions. Explanation is recognition; recognition is liberation.
column6:
Medicine of the soul — philosophy as therapeutic explanation

Ibn Sīnā's answer to the question of explanation is shaped by his identity as both philosopher and physician. Consciousness can be explained — through the faculty psychology inherited from Aristotle and transformed by his own analysis — and this explanation is practically valuable: understanding the soul's faculties, their proper objects, their disorders, and their remedies is the basis of both psychology and medicine. His account of the soul in *Kitāb al-Shifāʾ* is the most systematic medieval explanation of consciousness, covering sensation, imagination, estimation, memory, and intellect in rigorous detail. But Ibn Sīnā also recognizes a limit: the soul's self-knowledge — the immediate self-presence of the rational soul to itself — is not explicable in terms of more basic components. It is a fundamental datum. Explanation can account for the cognitive faculties that depend on the body; it cannot account for the soul's immediate self-awareness, which is given prior to and independent of all faculties. There is a hard core of consciousness that resists explanation from outside.
column7:
Complete in principle — but only God has the explanation

Leibniz's answer to whether consciousness can be explained is characteristically nuanced: yes, in principle — but the explanation is of infinite complexity and only God can grasp it fully. Every state of a monad follows necessarily from its previous states by its own internal law (*appetition* toward greater clarity of perception), and that law was established by God in accordance with the principle of sufficient reason. A complete explanation of any moment of consciousness would require tracing the entire causal history of the monad from its creation — an infinite regress that only an infinite intellect can hold. For finite minds, explanation is asymptotic: we can explain more and more by tracing more and more of the causal history, but we can never reach the complete explanation. The famous remark about the mill — if you enlarged the brain to the size of a mill and walked inside, you would find only levers and springs, never perception — expresses Leibniz's conviction that mechanical explanation of consciousness is in principle inadequate: no arrangement of matter can explain why there is perception, even though every perception has a complete causal explanation in the monad's internal law.
column8:
Psychology as natural science — explanation through functional analysis

James's answer to the question of explanation is pragmatic and pluralist. In the *Principles of Psychology*, he commits to a naturalistic psychology: consciousness is to be explained by studying the brain and its functions, not by armchair philosophy. But he is also deeply skeptical of the reductionist program: the attempt to explain consciousness by identifying it with neural processes (psychophysical identity theory) seems to him to miss the phenomenon entirely. His famous remark about the 'mind-stuff theory' — that it tries to build consciousness out of units that are not themselves conscious — anticipates Chalmers's hard problem by a century. James's pragmatic solution is to separate the question of how consciousness functions (which science can study) from the question of what consciousness fundamentally is (which remains deeply mysterious). The *Essays in Radical Empiricism* represent his later, more metaphysical attempt to resolve the mystery by treating pure experience as the fundamental category — neither mental nor physical but the neutral stuff from which both are constructed. This neutral monism is the most ambitious explanation James offers, and it remains unfinished.
column9:
Phenomenology as rigorous science — explanation through eidetic analysis

Husserl's answer to the question of explanation is that consciousness requires a new kind of science — phenomenology — that is rigorously scientific in method but not naturalistic in presupposition. The natural sciences explain by subsuming phenomena under causal laws; phenomenology explains by describing the essential structures of conscious experience as they appear to consciousness itself, after the bracketing (*epoché*) of all assumptions about the existence of an external world. This *eidetic analysis* yields universal, necessary truths about consciousness — truths that hold for any possible experience, regardless of the specific empirical facts about this particular brain or this particular organism. Husserl insists that naturalistic psychology cannot explain consciousness because it presupposes what needs to be explained: the intentional structure of consciousness that makes scientific observation itself possible. Phenomenology is therefore not an alternative to natural science but its foundation — the analysis of the conditions of possibility of any science whatsoever. Whether phenomenology constitutes an explanation in any sense that goes beyond description is a question Husserl's successors have debated endlessly.
column10:
Neural Darwinism — biological explanation without the hard problem dissolving

Edelman's answer to the question of explanation is the most confident naturalistic position in this table: consciousness can be fully explained by neuroscience, and the hard problem as Chalmers formulates it is a pseudo-problem that dissolves once you understand the biology correctly. His theory of Neural Darwinism — the selection of neuronal groups by value systems shaped by evolutionary history, generating the dynamic core that constitutes consciousness — is offered as a complete biological explanation of consciousness. Edelman is not an eliminativist: he insists that qualia are real and that consciousness is not the same as computation. But he believes that the felt quality of experience is the biological value system operating — it is what it is like, from the inside, for a system shaped by evolution to respond to a stimulus in a value-laden way. The explanatory gap is not a genuine metaphysical chasm but a reflection of our current ignorance of the biological details. Given a complete neuroscience, the gap would close. This puts Edelman in direct and explicit opposition to Chalmers, for whom the gap is principled rather than empirical.
column11:
The hard problem — explanation fails at the phenomenal level

Chalmers's answer to the question of explanation is the central contribution of his career: consciousness cannot be fully explained by any physical, functional, or neuroscientific account, because all such accounts leave out the phenomenal — the subjective character of experience. The argument is precise: any complete physical description of the brain specifies only structural and functional properties — which mechanisms do what, which processes cause which other processes. But phenomenal properties — what it is like to see red, to feel pain, to hear a minor chord — are not structural or functional. They are intrinsic, not relational; they are qualities, not dispositions. Therefore no functional account entails the existence of phenomenal properties; there is always a logical gap between the functional story and the phenomenal fact. This does not mean consciousness is supernatural or miraculous; it means that the categories of our current physical theory are insufficient. Chalmers's proposed solutions — property dualism, panpsychism, the principle of organizational invariance — all acknowledge the reality of the explanatory gap while attempting to give it a principled metaphysical home.

column1:
Ultimate

Ground of reality or late arrival?

Ultimate synthesis

Three positions. Consciousness as ground (Patanjali, Vasubandhu, Xuanzang, Abhinavagupta, Husserl): consciousness is not one thing among others but the condition of possibility of everything else — the eternal witness, the only reality, the absolute, or the transcendental constitution of all meaning. Consciousness as participant (Ibn Sīnā, Leibniz): consciousness is real and irreducible but not the ultimate ground; God is the necessary being in which souls and monads are grounded, and consciousness participates in divine rationality without constituting it. Consciousness as emergent (Edelman): the product of four billion years of biological evolution — the most complex process in the universe but not its ground; it arrived late and is not necessary. James's neutral monism and Chalmers's openness to panpsychism occupy a fourth position — dissolving the question rather than answering it by making experience or information the neutral matrix of which both mind and matter are configurations.
column2:
Puruṣa as the ground — consciousness prior to and beyond the cosmos

Patanjali's answer to the ultimate status of consciousness is unambiguous: puruṣa is one of the two ultimate principles of reality, and it is, of the two, the more fundamental in the sense of being the only truly self-sufficient one. Prakṛti (matter-energy) exists, but it exists for the sake of puruṣa — for the sake of puruṣa's experience and eventual liberation. The cosmos is a theater constructed for the education and ultimate liberation of consciousness. This makes consciousness, for Patanjali, not exactly the ground of all reality (as it is for Abhinavagupta) but certainly not a late and marginal arrival: it is one of the two irreducible foundations of everything that exists. The deepest metaphysical claim of the Yoga Sutras is that consciousness is not at home in matter — their association is contingent, not necessary — and that the final truth of things is a pure consciousness that has left matter behind and abides in its own nature, alone and sufficient.
column3:
Consciousness as the only reality — vijñānavāda

Vasubandhu's answer to the ultimate status of consciousness is the most uncompromising in this table: consciousness is the only reality. There is no matter, no external world, no physical substrate — only vijñāna, consciousness, in its various transformations. This is not an argument that the physical world is not real in a practical sense (the regularities of experience are perfectly real and must be respected); it is the metaphysical claim that when we ask what, at the most fundamental level, everything consists of, the answer is: representation, awareness, cognition. The universe is not a physical universe in which minds occasionally arise; it is a universe of minds — streams of consciousness — in which the appearance of a physical world is a structural feature of consciousness's own internal dynamics. This places Vasubandhu at the idealist extreme of the metaphysical spectrum, more radical than Berkeley (who preserved God as an external guarantor) and anticipating the most thoroughgoing forms of German Idealism.
column4:
Consciousness as the ultimate ground of all appearance

Xuanzang's ultimate metaphysics follows Vasubandhu's vijñānavāda but with a crucial refinement: the ultimate reality is not merely consciousness as we ordinarily know it (vijñāna in the sense of the eight consciousnesses) but consciousness in its purified, transformed state — the four wisdoms that arise when the eight vijñānas are liberated from the distortions of ignorance. The ultimate is not the ālayavijñāna (which is still conditioned, still a product of karmic seeds) but the buddha-nature (*tathāgatagarbha*, which Xuanzang's tradition carefully distinguishes from the ālaya) — the unconditional consciousness that is the ground of all possible appearance. This nuance is crucial: consciousness as it ordinarily operates is not ultimate; consciousness as it is when fully awakened to its own nature is ultimate. The cosmos is grounded in awareness, but in awareness at its most transparent, most liberated, most fully itself — not in the ordinary vijñāna of confused and suffering beings.
column5:
Śiva as pure cit — consciousness is not in reality, reality is in consciousness

Abhinavagupta's answer to the ultimate status of consciousness is the most expansive in this table: consciousness is not merely one feature of ultimate reality; it is identical with ultimate reality. Śiva — the absolute — is pure self-luminous, self-reflexive consciousness (*cit*). Everything that exists — every stone, every galaxy, every moment of experience, every mathematical truth — is a self-expression of this one absolute consciousness. There is no thing that is not consciousness; there is no moment that is not consciousness recognizing itself in some form. The cosmos is not a container in which consciousness occasionally appears; it is the self-display of consciousness in all its contracted and expanded forms. This is not subjective idealism (the world is my idea): it is absolute idealism, in which the absolute consciousness that is the ground of all things is not any individual mind but the source from which all individual minds, and all apparently non-mental things, equally derive. The astrophysicist's universe — with its billion galaxies and its indifferent physical laws — is, on this account, consciousness dreaming itself in a particularly elaborate and impersonal form.
column6:
Rational soul as microcosm — consciousness bridging matter and intellect

Ibn Sīnā's answer to the ultimate status of consciousness is shaped by his Neoplatonic cosmology: consciousness — specifically the rational soul — occupies the crucial middle position in the hierarchy of existence. Below the rational soul: the material world, governed by causal necessity, devoid of self-awareness. Above it: the separate Intellects (the intelligences of the celestial spheres), culminating in the Active Intellect (the lowest of the cosmic minds, the one closest to the human) and ultimately in the Necessary Being (God). The rational soul is the point at which matter achieves self-awareness and at which the chain of being folds back on itself: matter knowing itself through the soul's capacity for intellectual contemplation. Consciousness is therefore neither the ultimate ground of reality (God is not conscious in the way a mind is conscious; the Necessary Being is pure existence and pure necessity) nor a mere byproduct (it is the culmination of the material world's upward reach toward intellect). It is the universe knowing itself from the inside.
column7:
Monads as mirrors of God — consciousness as the universe's self-reflection

Leibniz's answer to the ultimate status of consciousness is one of the most beautiful in this table: every monad is a living mirror of the universe, and the universe itself — in God's infinite intellect — is a perfect self-reflection. Consciousness is not a late and marginal arrival in a material universe; consciousness is what the universe fundamentally consists of, since every substance is a monad and every monad is a perceiving thing. Matter, in the ordinary sense, is merely the confused appearance of an aggregate of monads — what the low-grade monads look like from the outside. The ultimate reality is the community of monads, each reflecting all others from its own unique perspective, all coordinated by the pre-established harmony of God's creation. The most exalted form of this universal mirroring is the rational soul's love of God: the soul that clearly and distinctly perceives the order of the universe, loves the God who established that order, and participates in God's own joy in the perfection of the creation. Consciousness is ultimately a participation in the divine self-knowledge — which is the universe knowing itself fully.
column8:
Pure experience as the neutral ground — consciousness and matter as aspects

James's later metaphysics — *Essays in Radical Empiricism* (1904–1905) — proposes what he calls *radical empiricism*: the view that the ultimate stuff of reality is neither mental nor physical but *pure experience* — a neutral, undivided flow of events from which both consciousness and matter are abstracted. Consciousness, on this view, is not a thing but a function: the same piece of pure experience can be counted as mental when taken in one relational context (as part of a stream of thought) and as physical when taken in another (as part of the experienced world). This neutral monism dissolves the mind-body problem by dissolving its terms: there is no mind distinct from matter, no matter distinct from mind — there is only experience, and the mental-physical distinction is a distinction in how we relate to the same experiential events. Whether consciousness is ultimate or derivative depends on whether pure experience is itself a form of awareness (which would make consciousness prior) or a pre-experiential substrate (which would make consciousness emergent). James leaves this question deliberately open — it is one of the most genuinely open questions in his philosophy.
column9:
Transcendental idealism — consciousness as the condition, not the content, of reality

Husserl's answer to the ultimate status of consciousness deepens throughout his career toward what he calls *transcendental idealism*: the view that the world as it is for us — the world of science, of everyday experience, of philosophical reflection — is constituted by consciousness. This is not the claim that the physical world does not exist; it is the claim that the world's *sense* — its being as the world it is — is given through the constitutive achievements of transcendental subjectivity. Consciousness is not a part of the world; it is the horizon within which any world can appear. The natural attitude (taking the world as a simply existing given) is a fundamental forgetting of the constitutive role of consciousness; phenomenology is the systematic recovering of that forgotten constitution. Husserl's transcendental idealism places consciousness as the condition of the possibility of any reality appearing as what it is — making consciousness ultimate not as a substance but as the irreplaceable medium through which reality has any sense at all.
column10:
A late arrival — consciousness as the universe knowing itself briefly

Edelman's answer to the ultimate status of consciousness is the most cosmologically humbling in this table: consciousness is a late, local, fragile achievement of biological evolution on one small planet in one ordinary galaxy. The universe was not built for consciousness; consciousness was built by the universe through the blind, purposeless process of natural selection. But this does not diminish consciousness — it makes it more remarkable. The dynamic core that generates the remembered present, that allows a mammal to construct a scene and an experience, that allows a human being to know that it knows, is the product of billions of years of evolutionary tinkering with neural matter. It is the universe becoming aware of itself — not by design, not as fulfillment of a cosmic purpose, but accidentally, contingently, temporarily. Edelman quotes Gerard Manley Hopkins with evident sympathy: consciousness is the universe 'charged with the grandeur of God' — not because God put it there, but because the self-knowing of the universe, however brief and however local, is the most extraordinary fact we know.
column11:
Panpsychism as the most coherent conclusion — consciousness woven into the fabric

Chalmers's answer to the ultimate status of consciousness is his most speculative and most significant: the combination of the hard problem argument (consciousness cannot be derived from physical processes) and the principle of organizational invariance (any system with the right kind of information-processing structure will be conscious) leads him toward *panpsychism* — the view that consciousness is a fundamental and ubiquitous feature of reality, not confined to biological nervous systems but present wherever there is information processing of the relevant kind. This is not the claim that rocks are conscious in the way humans are; it is the claim that there is something it is like to be any information-processing system, however simple, and that complex human consciousness is a high-level manifestation of something that is present throughout nature. Chalmers connects this to IIT (Integrated Information Theory, Tononi's framework) and to various forms of Russellian monism (the view that consciousness fills the intrinsic nature of physical reality, which physics describes only structurally). If Chalmers is right, the astrophysicist's universe is not indifferent to consciousness: it is saturated with it, from the simplest information-bearing interaction to the most complex self-reflective mind.
